text: Here we present a stepwise approach to laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with DII dissection. Our patient is a six year old male with the following medical history. The tumour can be visualised on this CT scan.

The patient is positioned supine, with surgical ports placed according to a diagram. After port placement, the patient is tilted head down and right shoulder up. The omentum is moved into the right upper quadrant above the liver, and the small bowel is positioned into the left side of the abdomen.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection of the ascending colon, focusing on separating the duodenum and controlling the vessels.

The surgeon begins dissection under the iliocolic pedicle, making an initial incision into the peritoneum. The dissection proceeds in a bloodless plane using hooked diathermy and blunt dissection techniques.
The surgeon progresses towards the duodenum, separating retroperitoneal structures and fascia from the mesocolon using precise dissection techniques.

The surgeon identifies and separates the duodenum from the mesocolon using scissors and blunt dissection, avoiding diathermy to prevent thermal injury. Important retroperitoneal structures such as the inferior vena cava (IVC), gonadal vessels, and ureter are clearly visible.

After fully mobilizing the duodenum, the surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection using a harmonic scalpel and gentle sweeping motions to separate the retroperitoneal fascia from the mesocolic envelope. A distinct color delineation is visible, marking the borders between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum.
A swab is placed above the duodenum to serve as a visual landmark for subsequent stages of the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon displays the mobilized duodenum, the head of the pancreas, and retroperitoneal structures that have been separated from the mesocolon, highlighting the completion of the dissection in this area.
The surgeon begins vessel control by addressing the ileocolic pedicle, preparing to manage the vascular structures in this area.

The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic pedicle and exposes the superior mesenteric vein by applying counter traction, pulling the pedicle to the patient's right side. This tension facilitates the division of peritoneal coverings. Haemlock clips are placed proximally and distally on the vessel, which is then divided between the clips. Additional clips are applied to nearby structures to minimize bleeding risk.
The surgeon demonstrates the control of the ileocolic artery and vein, which lie in close proximity to each other, using haemlock clips as shown in the earlier diagram.

The surgeon identifies and divides the tributaries of the superior mesenteric vein following the division of the ileocolic pedicle. Clips are applied as proximal as possible to the branches, ensuring central venous ligation and maximizing lymph node yield for optimal oncological outcomes.
The surgeon demonstrates the mobility gained from vessel control, allowing for dissection above the duodenum. A window into the lesser sac is created and visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon has completed the medial to lateral dissection, revealing the exposed duodenum and head of the pancreas. Final vessels, likely tributaries of the gastrocolonic trunk, are visible and can be safely ligated.

The surgeon elevates the cecum and appendix to create tension, facilitating entry into an avascular plane for dissection. Using traction and counter traction, the fibers between the posterior fascia and the mesocolic envelope of the cecum are divided, aiming to connect this dissection with the previously completed medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon carefully creates a window by joining the medial to lateral dissection with the subalial dissection, while avoiding injury to the posterior gonadal vessels and ureter. The proximity of these structures is visible, and the dissection continues until reaching the swab placed over the duodenum.

The assistant pulls the colon towards the patient's left side, creating tension that facilitates the lateral mobilisation of the colon. The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to dissect while preserving the intact mesocolic envelope.
The surgeon continues lateral mobilization of the colon until the right lobe of the liver becomes clearly visible, indicating the completion of this stage of the procedure.

The surgeon creates a window into the lesser sac between the colon and omentum, separating these structures. The dissection proceeds in a medial to lateral direction, joining the previously achieved lateral mobilisation line. With the colon fully mobilised, the surgeon divides the remaining vessels, likely the right gastroepiploic vein.

The colon is positioned at the patient's midline, revealing underlying posterior structures and haemlocks marking the centrally ligated vessels. The specimen is extracted through a small midline incision with an Alexis wound protector. A side-to-side anastomosis is performed using TLS-CMT and TA-CMT linear staplers, following the standard Barcelona technique.
